section.title: The Tribe of Judah
section.sectionRationale: Having surveyed all humanity in chapter 1, the Chronicler now narrows to Israel's twelve sons and, immediately, to Judah, the royal and messianic tribe. Three chapters trace Judah's lines: from Perez through Ram to Jesse and David; through Caleb and Jerahmeel; David's own sons and the royal line down through the exile and beyond; and further clans of Judah, including the brief but striking prayer of Jabez.
section.thesis: By devoting three whole chapters, far more than any other tribe receives, to Judah's tangled, sometimes scandalous, sometimes obscure family lines, the Chronicler shows the returned exiles that the royal line of promise survived every failure, every obscure branch, and even the exile itself, carried by God's hand from Judah through David toward the everlasting King still to come.
section.frameNote: Ten movements: the sons of Israel; Judah's sons through Tamar; the line of Perez to David; Caleb son of Hezron and the line to Bezalel; the line of Jerahmeel; the other line of Caleb from Hebron to the Kenites; the sons of David; the royal line from Solomon through the exile and after; more clans of Judah and the prayer of Jabez; and further clans and craftsmen of Judah.

verses.text: These were the sons of Israel: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun,
Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.

units.label: The sons of Judah through Tamar
units.note: a wicked firstborn and an unlikely mother

verses.text: The sons of Judah: Er, Onan, and Shelah. These three were born to him by Bathshua the Canaanite. Er, Judah's {{bekor|firstborn}}, was wicked in the sight of the LORD. So the LORD put him to death.
Tamar, Judah's daughter-in-law, bore to him Perez and Zerah. Judah had five sons in all.

units.label: The line of Perez to David
units.note: the royal thread from Judah's son to Jesse's youngest

verses.text: The sons of Perez: Hezron and Hamul.
The sons of Zerah: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara, five in all.
The son of Carmi: Achar, who brought trouble upon Israel by violating the {{cherem|ban}} on devoted things.
The son of Ethan: Azariah.
The sons who were born to Hezron: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Caleb.
Ram was the father of Amminadab, and Amminadab was the father of Nahshon, a leader of the {{zera|descendants}} of Judah.
Nahshon was the father of Salmon, and Salmon was the father of Boaz.
Boaz was the father of Obed, and Obed was the father of Jesse.
Jesse was the father of Eliab his firstborn; Abinadab was born second, Shimea third,
Nethanel fourth, Raddai fifth,
Ozem sixth, and David seventh.
Their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail. And the three sons of Zeruiah were Abishai, Joab, and Asahel.
Abigail was the mother of Amasa, whose father was Jether the Ishmaelite.

units.label: Caleb son of Hezron and the line to Bezalel

verses.text: Caleb son of Hezron had children by his wife Azubah and by Jerioth. These were the sons of Azubah: Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon.
When Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath, who bore to him Hur.
Hur was the father of Uri, and Uri was the father of Bezalel.
Later, Hezron slept with the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead. He had married her when he was sixty years old, and she bore to him Segub.
Segub was the father of Jair, who had twenty-three cities in the land of Gilead.
But Geshur and Aram captured Havvoth-jair, along with Kenath and its sixty surrounding villages. All these were descendants of Machir the father of Gilead.
After Hezron died in Caleb-ephrathah, his wife Abijah bore to him Ashhur the father of Tekoa.

units.label: The line of Jerahmeel
units.note: a lesser-known branch, still recorded in full

verses.text: The sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron: Ram his firstborn, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah.
Jerahmeel had another wife named Atarah, who was the mother of Onam.
The sons of Ram the firstborn of Jerahmeel: Maaz, Jamin, and Eker.
The sons of Onam: Shammai and Jada. The sons of Shammai: Nadab and Abishur.
Abishur's wife was named Abihail, and she bore to him Ahban and Molid.
The sons of Nadab: Seled and Appaim. Seled died without children.
The son of Appaim: Ishi. The son of Ishi: Sheshan. The son of Sheshan: Ahlai.
The sons of Jada the brother of Shammai: Jether and Jonathan. Jether died without children.
The sons of Jonathan: Peleth and Zaza. These were the descendants of Jerahmeel.
Sheshan had no sons, but only daughters. He also had an Egyptian servant named Jarha.
Sheshan gave his daughter in marriage to his servant Jarha, and she bore to him Attai.
Attai was the father of Nathan, Nathan was the father of Zabad,
Zabad was the father of Ephlal, Ephlal was the father of Obed,
Obed was the father of Jehu, Jehu was the father of Azariah,
Azariah was the father of Helez, Helez was the father of Elasah,
Elasah was the father of Sismai, Sismai was the father of Shallum,
Shallum was the father of Jekamiah, and Jekamiah was the father of Elishama.

units.label: The other line of Caleb: Hebron to the Kenites
units.note: clans, craftsmen, and even outsiders folded into Judah

verses.text: The sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel: Mesha his firstborn, who was the father of Ziph, and Mareshah his second son, who was the father of Hebron.
The sons of Hebron: Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema.
Shema was the father of Raham the father of Jorkeam, and Rekem was the father of Shammai.
The son of Shammai was Maon, and Maon was the father of Beth-zur.
Caleb's concubine Ephah was the mother of Haran, Moza, and Gazez. Haran was the father of Gazez.
The sons of Jahdai: Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph.
Caleb's concubine Maacah was the mother of Sheber and Tirhanah.
She was also the mother of Shaaph father of Madmannah, and of Sheva father of Machbenah and Gibea. Caleb's daughter was Acsah.
These were the descendants of Caleb. The sons of Hur the firstborn of Ephrathah: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim,
Salma the father of Bethlehem, and Hareph the father of Beth-gader.
These were the descendants of Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim: Haroeh, half the {{mishpachah|Manahathites}},
and the clans of Kiriath-jearim, the Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, and Mishraites. From these descended the Zorathites and Eshtaolites.
The descendants of Salma: Bethlehem, the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, half the Manahathites, the Zorites,
and the clans of the scribes who lived at Jabez, the Tirathites, Shimeathites, and Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath, the father of the house of Rechab.

units.label: The sons of David
units.note: born in Hebron and in Jerusalem

verses.text: These were the sons of David who were born to him in Hebron: The firstborn was Amnon by Ahinoam of Jezreel; the second was Daniel by Abigail of Carmel;
the third was Absalom the son of Maacah daughter of King Talmai of Geshur; the fourth was Adonijah the son of Haggith;
the fifth was Shephatiah by Abital; and the sixth was Ithream by his wife Eglah.
These six sons were born to David in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months. And David reigned in Jerusalem thirty-three years,
and these sons were born to him in Jerusalem: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon. These four were born to him by Bathsheba daughter of Ammiel.
David's other sons were Ibhar, Elishua, Eliphelet,
Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia,
Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet, nine in all.
These were all the sons of David, besides the sons by his concubines. And Tamar was their sister.

units.label: The royal line from Solomon through the exile and after
units.note: the throne survives even the captivity

verses.text: Solomon's son was Rehoboam: Abijah was his son, Asa his son, Jehoshaphat his son,
Joram his son, Ahaziah his son, Joash his son,
Amaziah his son, Azariah his son, Jotham his son,
Ahaz his son, Hezekiah his son, Manasseh his son,
Amon his son, and Josiah his son.
The sons of Josiah: Johanan was the firstborn, Jehoiakim the second, Zedekiah the third, and Shallum the fourth.
The successors of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah his son, and Zedekiah.
The descendants of Jeconiah the captive: Shealtiel his son,
Malchiram, Pedaiah, Shenazzar, Jekamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah.
The sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel and Shimei. The children of Zerubbabel: Meshullam and Hananiah, their sister Shelomith,
and five others: Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, and Jushab-hesed.
The descendants of Hananiah: Pelatiah, Jeshaiah, and the sons of Rephaiah, of Arnan, of Obadiah, and of Shecaniah.
The six descendants of Shecaniah were Shemaiah and his sons: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat.
The sons of Neariah: Elioenai, Hizkiah, and Azrikam, three in all.
The sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, and Anani, seven in all.

units.label: More clans of Judah and the prayer of Jabez
units.note: an honored man cries out for blessing

verses.text: The descendants of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal.
Reaiah son of Shobal was the father of Jahath, and Jahath was the father of Ahumai and Lahad. These were the {{mishpachah|clans}} of the Zorathites.
These were the sons of Etam: Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash. And their sister was named Hazzelelponi.
Penuel was the father of Gedor, and Ezer was the father of Hushah. These were the descendants of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah and the father of Bethlehem.
Ashhur the father of Tekoa had two wives, Helah and Naarah.
Naarah bore to him Ahuzzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari. These were the descendants of Naarah.
The sons of Helah were Zereth, Zohar, Ethnan,
and Koz, who was the father of Anub and Zobebah and of the clans of Aharhel son of Harum.
Now Jabez was more honorable than his brothers. His mother had named him Jabez, saying, "Because I bore him in pain."

And Jabez called out to the God of Israel, "If only You would bless me and enlarge my territory! May Your hand be with me and keep me from harm, so that I will be free from pain." And God granted the request of Jabez.

units.label: Further clans and craftsmen of Judah
units.note: linen workers, potters, and rulers in Moab

verses.text: Chelub the brother of Shuhah was the father of Mehir, who was the father of Eshton.
Eshton was the father of Beth-rapha, of Paseah, and of Tehinnah the father of Ir-nahash. These were the men of Recah.
The sons of Kenaz: Othniel and Seraiah. The sons of Othniel: Hathath and Meonothai.
Meonothai was the father of Ophrah, and Seraiah was the father of Joab, the father of those living in Ge-harashim, which was given this name because its people were craftsmen.
The sons of Caleb son of Jephunneh: Iru, Elah, and Naam. The son of Elah: Kenaz.
The sons of Jehallelel: Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel.
The sons of Ezrah: Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon. And Mered's wife Bithiah gave birth to Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah the father of Eshtemoa.
These were the children of Pharaoh's daughter Bithiah. Mered also took a Judean wife, who gave birth to Jered the father of Gedor, Heber the father of Soco, and Jekuthiel the father of Zanoah.
The sons of Hodiah's wife, the sister of Naham, were the fathers of Keilah the Garmite and of Eshtemoa the Maacathite.
The sons of Shimon: Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-hanan, and Tilon. The descendants of Ishi: Zoheth and Ben-zoheth.
The sons of Shelah son of Judah: Er the father of Lecah, Laadah the father of Mareshah and the clans of the linen workers at Beth-ashbea,
Jokim, the men of Cozeba, and Joash and Saraph, who ruled in Moab and Jashubi-lehem. (These names are from ancient records.)
These were the potters who lived at Netaim and Gederah. They lived there in the service of the king.

bekor.gloss: Firstborn, with the double portion of inheritance and family leadership that normally belonged to him. Judah's actual firstborn, Er, was struck dead for wickedness, and the line of promise instead ran through Perez, born out of a scandalous encounter with Tamar, showing again that God's blessing follows His choice rather than birth order.

zera.gloss: Seed, offspring, descendants. Nahshon, a leader of Judah's descendants during the exodus generation, stands in the very line that runs through Boaz, Jesse, and David, and at last, in the New Testament's reckoning, to Jesus, the promised seed of Abraham and son of David.

cherem.gloss: The ban, devotion to destruction, things set apart wholly for the LORD that no Israelite might take for personal use. Achar, called Achan in Joshua 7, violated this ban after the fall of Jericho and brought disaster on all Israel, a stark reminder within Judah's own family tree that hidden sin has public consequences.

mishpachah.gloss: Clan or extended family, the middle layer of Israelite identity between the individual household and the whole tribe. The Chronicler repeatedly names these clans, of Zorah, of Manahath, and others, because belonging to a mishpachah was how an ordinary Israelite, and an ordinary returned exile, knew exactly where they fit within Judah and within Israel as a whole.

basic.context: Having surveyed the whole human race in chapter one, the Chronicler now narrows to Israel's twelve sons and gives by far the most space, three full chapters, to Judah. Judah is the royal, messianic tribe, and its family tree is traced from Perez, born to Tamar, all the way to Jesse and David, then through the kings of Judah down to the exile, and even into the generations after the return. Along the way the Chronicler pauses over striking figures: Achar, who brought disaster on Israel; Caleb, the faithful spy; Bezalel, the tabernacle's craftsman; and Jabez, a man who cried out to God for blessing and received it. A reader naturally asks why Judah gets so much space, what these scattered, sometimes obscure names have to do with each other, and what the little story of Jabez is doing tucked inside a long genealogy.
basic.hermeneutics: This is genealogical record punctuated by a few short narrative asides, most notably Achar's sin (2:7) and Jabez's prayer (4:9-10). The genealogical form itself makes a theological point: by tracing every branch faithfully, even obscure ones, the Chronicler shows that God's providence over Judah's royal line was not a single dramatic act but a patient, generation-by-generation preservation, through faithful men, failed men, foreign wives, and even a captivity, of the line that would produce David and, later readers would come to see, David's greater Son.
basic.originalLanguages: Judah's story turns on bekor, the birthright, which twice reverses itself: Er the actual firstborn dies for wickedness, and Perez, born through Tamar's desperate stratagem, carries the royal line forward instead. Nahshon is called a leader of the zera, the descendants, of Judah, a word that later narrows to the one promised seed, Christ. Achar's sin is described with the word cherem, the ban, devoted things that were not his to take, a warning embedded right inside the ancestry of Israel's kings. And throughout, the mishpachah, the clan, is the basic unit by which ordinary Israelites, and later ordinary exiles reading this list, located their own place in the tribe.
basic.typology: Tamar, an outsider drawn into Judah's family through a desperate and shaming act, becomes an ancestress of David and later appears by name in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus, foreshadowing how God's grace works through broken and unlikely circumstances to advance His saving plan. Bezalel, filled by God's Spirit to build the tabernacle, prefigures the greater temple-builder, Christ, in whom God's presence finally dwells among His people without a curtain.
basic.christ: This chapter's long central line, Perez, Hezron, Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon, Boaz, Obed, Jesse, David, is the very spine of Matthew's genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:3-6), and Tamar, so prominent here, is one of only a few women Matthew names in that genealogy. The royal line then continues past David through Solomon, all the way to Jeconiah the captive and Zerubbabel after the exile (3:17-19), the same Zerubbabel Matthew names as an ancestor of Jesus (Matthew 1:12-13). The exile could have ended the Davidic line forever; instead, the Chronicler shows the line surviving captivity intact, evidence that God's promise to David of an everlasting throne, 2 Samuel 7:16, was never actually broken, only hidden, until it flowered in Jesus, the Son of David.
basic.god: God is shown here overturning human expectation at almost every turn: choosing Perez over the expected firstborn line, working through Tamar's desperate and shameful circumstances, judging Achar's secret sin publicly, granting Jabez's bold request for blessing, and preserving David's royal line even through the disaster of exile and captivity. Nothing about Judah's history looks tidy or obviously destined for greatness, yet God's hand runs through every generation of it toward the throne He had promised David forever.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 38 tells the fuller, scandalous story of Judah, Tamar, and the birth of Perez and Zerah. Joshua 7 narrates Achan's (Achar's) sin at Jericho in full. Ruth 4:18-22 supplies the same Perez-to-David line in a different literary setting. Matthew 1:1-16 builds its genealogy of Jesus on exactly this Judah-to-David thread, naming Tamar along the way.
basic.oneStory: Three long chapters trace Judah's tangled family from Tamar's scandal, through Achar's sin, Caleb's faithfulness, Jabez's prayer, David's throne, and finally through the devastation of exile, without the thread of promise ever actually breaking. For a community newly returned from that same exile, seeing their royal line still intact on the page was proof that God's covenant with David had survived every failure of the house of Judah, a promise that would find its full and final answer in Jesus Christ, Son of David, born of this same tangled, gracious, unlikely family line.

context.literary: Judah receives more space in these opening genealogies, three chapters, than any other tribe, a deliberate literary signal of its importance to the book's larger concerns: the Davidic monarchy and the Jerusalem temple. The section is structured around several branching lines from Hezron's three sons, Jerahmeel, Ram, and Caleb, so that the reader moves from Perez's royal line (2:9-17), to Caleb's line (2:18-24, 42-55), to Jerahmeel's line (2:25-41), before the narrative pauses for David's own sons (chapter 3) and the miniature scene of Jabez's prayer (4:9-10), the only extended narrative moment in the whole genealogical section.
context.historical: By the time Chronicles was compiled, the Davidic monarchy had been extinguished for generations, and the returned community lived under Persian rule with a Davidic descendant, Zerubbabel, functioning only as a provincial governor, not a king. Tracing Judah's royal line in such detail, especially past the exile in 3:17-24 to Zerubbabel's own children, quietly asserted that the Davidic family still existed, was still identifiable, and therefore the promise of an everlasting throne had not lapsed even though no descendant currently reigned.
context.canonical: This section supplies the genealogical backbone that both Ruth 4:18-22 and Matthew 1:1-17 depend on, tracing the line from Judah's son Perez through Boaz to David and onward. The prayer of Jabez, though only two verses, has become one of the most quoted passages from Chronicles in later devotional history, precisely because it interrupts the genealogical monotony with a real cry of faith that God answers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish, in careful and undeniable genealogical detail, that Judah's royal line, running from Perez through David and surviving even the Babylonian exile into Zerubbabel's own children, remains intact and identifiable, giving the restored community concrete grounds to hope that God's covenant promise to David had not lapsed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter records specific family history; it does not prescribe genealogical practice, nor does it hold up every figure named as a moral example, Achar being an explicit warning rather than a model. What it teaches enduringly is that God works out His covenant purposes through real, flawed families across many generations, and that no failure within that family, not Er's wickedness, not Achar's sin, not the exile itself, was able to end the line of promise.

debates.question: How should readers understand the brief, striking story of Jabez inserted into an otherwise unbroken genealogy?

views.label: A model of bold, God-centered prayer
views.case: Jabez, whose very name recalls the pain of his birth, refuses to be defined by his painful beginning and instead calls on 'the God of Israel' for blessing, enlarged territory, God's hand, and protection from harm; his prayer is immediately answered, making him an example of turning to God rather than resignation or self-reliance in the face of a hard start in life.

views.label: A caution against detaching the prayer from its context and turning it into a formula
views.case: Because the prayer sits inside Judah's genealogy, part of the royal line's larger story, some caution against lifting it out as a stand-alone prosperity formula to be repeated for personal gain; read in context, it is one honored man's specific, humble plea to the covenant God of Israel, not a guaranteed method for acquiring blessing, and its lasting lesson is the character of dependent, earnest prayer rather than a technique.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that Jabez's prayer is a genuine, answered act of faith worth learning from; the caution is simply against treating two verses tucked inside a genealogy as an isolated key to blessing, rather than an example of the kind of humble, God-ward asking every believer is invited to bring.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler include Achar's sin (2:7) inside the otherwise celebratory genealogy of Judah's royal line?

views.label: As an honest warning within the family of promise
views.case: Even Judah's honored line contains a man whose secret sin against the ban brought disaster on all Israel, Joshua 7; including it here refuses to sanitize the royal tribe's history, showing that being part of the chosen line is no guarantee against sin or its public consequences.

views.label: As a foil that highlights God's grace toward the rest of the line
views.case: Set beside Achar's judgment, the survival and flourishing of the rest of Judah's line, through Tamar, Ruth, and on to David, shows all the more clearly that the line's continuation depended on God's grace and choice rather than on the unbroken righteousness of every member of the family.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the note is not incidental: it is a deliberate reminder that God's covenant purposes advance through real, sinful people and real consequences, not through an idealized or airbrushed family history.

study.originalLanguages: The whole section turns on bekor, the birthright: Er, Judah's actual firstborn, is struck down for wickedness, and the royal line instead runs through Perez, born through Tamar. Nahshon is called a leader of the zera, the descendants, of Judah, the very word that later Scripture narrows to the one promised seed, fulfilled in Christ, Galatians 3:16. Achar's sin is described using cherem, the ban, devoted things set apart for the LORD that were not his to touch, a sober note embedded in an otherwise celebratory royal genealogy. And the repeated mishpachah, clan, groupings, of Zorah, of Manahath, of the linen workers, show how ordinary Israelites, and later ordinary exiles, located their place within the larger tribe through these smaller family units.

typology.type: Tamar, an outsider drawn into Judah's line through scandal and courage
typology.fulfillment: Christ's genealogy in Matthew 1 names Tamar among the mothers of the Messiah, showing that God's grace works through broken circumstances to advance His saving purposes.

typology.type: Bezalel, filled with the Spirit to build the tabernacle where God would dwell
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true tabernacle in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily and through whom God's presence comes to His people, John 1:14 and Colossians 2:9.

typology.type: The Davidic line surviving intact through the Babylonian exile
typology.fulfillment: The everlasting throne promised to David realized in Jesus, the Son of David, whose kingdom, unlike Judah's earthly monarchy, has no end, Luke 1:32-33.

study.christ: The central line of this section, Perez, Hezron, Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon, Boaz, Obed, Jesse, David, is precisely the spine of Matthew's genealogy of Jesus, Matthew 1:3-6, and Tamar, so prominently named here, is one of the very few women Matthew includes in that royal genealogy. The line does not stop at David; it continues past the exile to Jeconiah the captive and to Zerubbabel, 3:17-19, the same Zerubbabel Matthew names as an ancestor of Jesus, Matthew 1:12-13. Judah's history includes a wicked firstborn struck down, a scandal with a Canaanite daughter-in-law, a man judged for secret sin, and a captivity that could have ended everything, yet the line survives intact into the very genealogy the Gospels use to identify Jesus as the true Son of David, in whom God's covenant with David finally and fully comes to rest.
study.god: God's providence runs through every irregular branch of this family tree: through Tamar's desperate stratagem, through Caleb's faithful courage, through Bezalel's Spirit-given craftsmanship, through Achar's judged sin, through Jabez's bold and answered prayer, and through the Davidic line's survival past the catastrophe of exile into Zerubbabel's own children. None of it looks like a straight line of unbroken success; all of it is, in fact, the record of God patiently keeping His covenant promise to David alive through generations of ordinary, flawed people.

crossReferences.note: The fuller narrative behind 2:3-4, Judah, Tamar, and the birth of Perez and Zerah.
The full account of Achan's (Achar's) sin against the ban and its consequences for Israel, behind 2:7.
The same Perez-to-David genealogical line given in the closing verses of Ruth.
Matthew's genealogy of Jesus builds directly on this Judah-to-David-to-Zerubbabel line, naming Tamar among the mothers of the Messiah.

study.oneStory: Judah's family tree, traced across three long chapters, holds together scandal and faithfulness, judgment and answered prayer, monarchy and captivity, without the thread of promise ever finally snapping. For exiles just home from Babylon, seeing David's line recorded intact past the very exile they had endured was tangible proof that God's covenant with David still held. That thread runs on, through Zerubbabel and beyond, until it reaches Jesus Christ, Son of David, in whom the everlasting throne promised to Judah's royal line at last and forever comes to rest.

quiz.q: Why does the royal line of Judah run through Perez rather than through Er, Judah's actual firstborn?

options.text: Er was wicked and put to death by the LORD, so the line of promise instead ran through Perez, born to Tamar, showing God's blessing follows His choice rather than birth order.
options.note: 1 Chronicles 2:3-5 records Er's death and the continuation of the line through Perez, Tamar's son.

options.text: Perez was simply older than Er and Onan.
options.note: Perez was born after Er and Onan had already died; the reversal is precisely the point.

options.text: The line passed to Perez only because Er and Onan left no historical record at all.
options.note: The text explicitly states Er was wicked and put to death, and Onan is likewise mentioned; the reason given is moral, not merely a lack of records.

options.text: Judah's sons were listed in the order in which the Chronicler simply preferred to write them.
options.note: The order does follow birth, but the theological point is the reversal caused by Er's judgment, not the listing convention itself.

quiz.q: What does the placement of Achar's sin (2:7) inside Judah's celebrated genealogy communicate?

options.text: Even within the honored royal tribe, sin has real and public consequences, and the Chronicler refuses to hide it from the record.
options.note: The verse names Achar's violation of the ban as bringing trouble upon all Israel, an honest note within an otherwise celebratory family history.

options.text: That Judah's whole tribe was under a permanent curse because of Achar.
options.note: The text records Achar's individual sin and its consequence, not a perpetual curse on the whole tribe, which continues to flourish afterward.

options.text: That the Chronicler made a factual mistake by including this note.
options.note: The note matches the fuller account in Joshua 7 and is a deliberate inclusion, not an error.

options.text: That sin had occurred somewhere in Judah's history, without further significance.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but understates the honest, warning function of naming a specific sin within an otherwise honored genealogy.

quiz.q: What did Jabez ask God for, and how did God respond?

options.text: Jabez asked for blessing, enlarged territory, God's hand with him, and protection from harm, and God granted his request.
options.note: 1 Chronicles 4:10 records the specific prayer and its explicit answer.

options.text: Jabez asked only to be relieved of his painful name, and God changed his name.
options.note: His name is not changed in the text; his prayer concerns blessing, territory, God's presence, and protection.

options.text: Jabez prayed for personal wealth above all else, and God made him rich.
options.note: Blessing and enlarged territory are part of the request, but this option narrows and slightly distorts the fuller, more relational prayer for God's hand and protection.

options.text: Jabez prayed, but the text does not say whether God answered.
options.note: The text explicitly states, 'And God granted the request of Jabez.'

quiz.q: How does the royal line's survival past the Babylonian exile (3:17-24) matter for the book's first readers?

options.text: It shows David's line was not extinguished by the exile, giving concrete evidence that God's covenant promise of an everlasting throne still held.
options.note: The genealogy continues through Jeconiah the captive to Zerubbabel and his children, demonstrating the line's survival.

options.text: It shows the line ended completely with Jeconiah's captivity.
options.note: The opposite is true; the text explicitly continues the line past Jeconiah to Zerubbabel and beyond.

options.text: It is simply an interesting historical footnote with no bearing on the community's hope.
options.note: For a community that had just endured that same exile, this was far more than a footnote; it was tangible reassurance.

options.text: It shows the Davidic family survived, though the passage does not connect this to any wider hope.
options.note: True in itself, but this understates how directly this survival grounded hope for the fulfillment of God's covenant promise to David.

quiz.q: How does Judah's genealogy in this section connect to Jesus Christ in the New Testament?

options.text: The Perez-to-David line traced here, including Tamar, is the very backbone of Matthew's genealogy of Jesus as the Son of David.
options.note: Matthew 1:3-6 and 1:12-13 draw directly on this same line, including Tamar and Zerubbabel.

options.text: There is no meaningful connection between this genealogy and the New Testament.
options.note: Matthew's genealogy of Jesus is built directly on this material.

options.text: Only David himself, and no one else in this list, appears again in the New Testament.
options.note: Tamar, Boaz, and Zerubbabel, among others named here, all reappear in Matthew's genealogy.

options.text: The line shows Jesus descended from Judah, though the specific names are not repeated in the Gospels.
options.note: The specific names, including Tamar, Perez, Boaz, and Zerubbabel, are in fact repeated in Matthew's genealogy.

questions: Judah's family line includes scandal, sin, and obscure, easily forgotten branches, yet God worked through all of it toward David and, at last, toward Christ. Where in your own family story or personal history do you find it hard to believe God could still be at work?

Jabez refused to let a painful beginning define his future; instead he turned directly to God and asked boldly for blessing. What painful beginning in your life might God be inviting you to bring honestly and boldly before Him in prayer?

Tamar was an outsider drawn into Judah's family through desperate and shaming circumstances, yet she is honored by name in the genealogy of the Messiah. Where do you need to trust that God can use even your most painful or shameful circumstances for good?

The Davidic line survived the catastrophe of exile intact, giving the returned community real hope that God's promises still held. What circumstances in your life feel like they have broken something beyond repair, and how does God's faithfulness across Judah's generations speak to that fear?

Achar's hidden sin brought public trouble on all Israel, a sober note inside an otherwise celebrated family line. Is there anything hidden in your own life that you need to bring into the light before God rather than let it fester unseen?

translationNotes.note: The source text contained an em dash before 'five in all,' rendered here as a comma; most Hebrew manuscripts read Dara, while some manuscripts and the Syriac (compare 1 Kings 4:31) read Darda.
Achar means troubler and is the same man called Achan in Joshua 7 and Joshua 22; the BSB preserves the Chronicler's own spelling, which plays on the trouble he caused.
An em dash in the source before 'the Ithrites' was rendered here as a comma.
An em dash in the source before 'nine in all' was rendered here as a comma.
Em dashes in the source before 'three in all' and 'seven in all' were rendered here as commas.
The BSB footnote explains that the name Jabez sounds like the Hebrew word for pain or distress, the very meaning his mother named him for.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 2:1-55, 3:1-24, and 4:1-23. Several em dashes in the source (2:6, 2:53, 3:8, 3:23, 3:24) were converted to commas; none remain in this file.
